segments.title: Sasebo's island waterways as a fighting environment
segments.summary: A cruise through the islands around Sasebo shows why confined channels, visible rocks, and restricted movement make the local geography a difficult naval fighting environment.
segments.body: The harbour cruise passes through the islands around Sasebo on Kyushu, offering a practical view of a crowded coastal operating area. Dr. Clarke notes that battles have taken place in these waters, but says he would avoid choosing such terrain for a fight if possible. The narrow, obstructed waterways could favour close-range fighting while making movement and navigation difficult. He particularly stresses the danger for submarines, since shallow water and visible rocks leave little margin for a submerged vessel. The exchange illustrates how local geography can constrain the weapons, speed, and manoeuvre options available to a naval force.

evidence.note: The speakers describe the island cruise, the area's battle history, constrained fighting space, and the visible rocks that make it hazardous for submarines.
The discussion contrasts firepower with the speed and manoeuvrability needed to get through the confined waters.
